definition: A *treatise* is a formal, sustained written argument that handles a single subject in full. Where a *manuscript* names a book by how it was made and a *bestiary* by what it collects, a treatise is defined by its method — systematic exposition — and it is the characteristic form of learned magic, from Agrippa's three books to Thabit's *De Imaginibus*.
history: From Old French *traitis*, from Latin *tractare*, 'to handle, manage' (frequentative of *trahere*, 'to drag'). To write a treatise is, by the root, to 'handle' a topic thoroughly — the same *tractare* behind *treat*, *tract*, and *traction*. The turn is how the form dominates the magical archive: Agrippa's *De Occulta Philosophia* is the treatise that synthesized Renaissance magic, the *Picatrix* is a treatise on astral magic, and scholars measure such works by how much of the page the principal treatise occupies against its glosses.

def_sources.label: Attrell & Porreca, Picatrix: A Medieval Treatise on Astral Magic

etymology.origin: Old French traitis, from Latin tractare 'to handle' (trahere 'to drag')
etymology.note: A 'handling' of a subject; the same tractare as 'treat', 'tract', and 'traction' — an argument dragged out in full.
etymology.hook: To 'treat' a topic is to drag it out.

flavor_snippets.html: Afterward, a number of <mark>treatises</mark> on sea serpent morphology were swiftly revised.
